children.name: Sturnus
children.description: European starlings — extraordinarily social, adaptable, and vocally accomplished passerines that were once one of the most numerous farmland birds in northern Europe. The common starling (stare) is famous above all for its murmurations: in autumn, flocks of tens of thousands — sometimes hundreds of thousands — perform breathtaking coordinated aerial displays over Swedish wetlands, particularly at pre-roost gatherings in reedbeds. The intricate, unpredictable shape-shifting of murmuration flocks — a phenomenon studied by physicists using techniques from statistical mechanics — emerges from each bird following just six or seven of its nearest neighbours, with no central coordination. The starling is also one of Sweden's greatest mimics, incorporating the calls of other species, mechanical sounds, and even human speech into its continuous, churring song. Despite its familiarity, the starling is a rapidly declining farmland species throughout Sweden and Europe.

children.name: Sturnus vulgaris
children.description: The stare — one of Sweden's most familiar and yet rapidly declining birds. Its iridescent purple-green plumage, speckled with white 'stars' in winter, is beautiful at close range. In autumn, murmurations of hundreds of thousands of birds gather at Swedish wetland roosts — a sight of breathtaking scale and choreographic precision. Nests in tree holes and nest boxes throughout Sweden from April. An outstanding vocal mimic: Swedish starlings have been recorded imitating curlews, lapwings, and even mobile phone ringtones. Farmland populations have crashed by over 50% since 1980.

children.name: Sturnus unicolor
children.description: The spotless starling of Iberia and northwest Africa is the close western relative of the common starling, differing in its uniformly glossy black plumage lacking the pale spotting prominent in winter Sturnus vulgaris. It is a year-round resident across Spain, Portugal, and north Africa, rarely migrating. A colonial nester in cliff crevices, old buildings, and tree cavities, it forms large pre-roost gatherings in autumn that mirror the murmurations of its commoner relative.

children.name: Sturnus cineraceus
children.description: The white-cheeked starling breeds across northeast Asia from Siberia and China to Japan, migrating south in winter to Southeast Asia. Its grey-brown body is offset by a white cheek patch and reddish-orange bill base, giving it a distinctive appearance among Asian Sturnus. A familiar bird of parks, farmland, and woodland edges, it forms large winter flocks and readily associates with other starling species.

children.name: Sturnus burmannicus
children.description: The vinous-breasted starling inhabits dry deciduous woodlands and open country from Myanmar and Thailand to Indochina. Its creamy-vinous underparts contrast with a grey back and dark wings, and in breeding plumage males acquire a striking blue-grey hood. It is a gregarious species, foraging in flocks in open fields and nesting colonially in tree cavities and among rocky outcrops.

children.name: Sturnus eremicus
children.description: A starling of the arid coastal plains of Somalia and Djibouti; grey-brown with a pale belly and a black mask. Often found foraging on the ground near livestock.

children.name: Sturnus neglectus
children.description: A medium-sized starling of the Middle East and Central Asia, with a notably glossy green and purple sheen. The specific name reflects its former obscurity when treated as a subspecies of the common starling.

children.name: Sturnus opacus
children.description: A winter visitor to the Indian subcontinent from Central Asia. Its plumage is more uniformly dark than that of the European starling, lacking the white spots of the breeding plumage.

children.name: Sturnus caucasicus
children.description: A large, purple-glossed starling of the Caucasus and northern Iran. Its plumage has a notably strong purple sheen compared to the green gloss of the European starling.

children.name: Sturnus sericeus
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Sturnus philippensis
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Sturnus sturninus
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Sturnus malabaricus
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Sturnus pagodarum
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Sturnus erythropygius
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Sturnus nigricollis
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Sturnus sinensis
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Sturnus senex
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Pastor
children.description: Rosy starlings — striking, irruptive starlings of the central Asian steppes whose periodic westward irruptions bring them to Sweden as rare but unmistakeable vagrants. The rosy starling (rosenstare) is one of the most immediately recognisable birds on a Swedish list: its vivid combination of bubblegum pink body and glossy black head, wings, and tail makes it impossible to confuse with anything else. Like many nomadic steppe birds it irrupts westward irregularly, following locust swarms that erupt on the Eurasian steppes — the birds follow the locusts and can appear anywhere in western Europe and Scandinavia during such events. It is one of the most sought-after vagrants for Swedish birdwatchers, and years of irruption can bring tens of individuals to the country.

children.name: Pastor roseus
children.description: The rosenstare — one of the most spectacular and sought-after vagrant birds to appear in Sweden. Its unmistakeable bubblegum-pink and glossy-black plumage makes it stand out immediately in any flock of common starlings, which it associates with freely. Breeds on rocky steppe hillsides from southeastern Europe to Kazakhstan; follows locust swarms west during irruption years, appearing in Sweden at irregular intervals, most often in May–June. During major irruption events, dozens of individuals may arrive in Sweden simultaneously, causing considerable excitement among Swedish birders.

children.name: Acridotheres
children.description: Mynas — robust, conspicuous starlings of South and Southeast Asia renowned for their intelligence, bold temperament, and exceptional vocal abilities. The common myna is among the most successful bird species on the planet, having been introduced to dozens of countries worldwide and listed among the world's 100 worst invasive alien species. Mynas are aggressive competitors for nest cavities and food resources, yet also serve important ecological roles as seed dispersers and insect controllers. Their loud, varied calls and capacity for mimicry have made them popular cage birds for millennia.

children.name: Acridotheres tristis
children.description: The common myna is one of the world's most widespread and recognisable birds, native to South Asia but introduced across Africa, the Middle East, Australia, and numerous Pacific and Indian Ocean islands. Its brown body, black hood, yellow bill and bare facial skin, and bold white wing patches in flight are instantly distinctive. Listed among the world's 100 worst invasive species by the IUCN, it competes aggressively with native hole-nesting birds and has contributed to population declines of several island endemics.

children.name: Acridotheres ginginianus
children.description: The bank myna is endemic to the northern Indian subcontinent, from Pakistan through India to Bangladesh, inhabiting open country, cultivation, and river banks where it nests in earthen embankments and road cuttings. Slightly smaller than the common myna, it is distinguished by an orange-red facial patch (rather than yellow) and a more extensive white wing panel. It is a bold and adaptable forager, frequently following cattle and tractors to catch disturbed invertebrates.

children.name: Acridotheres fuscus
children.description: The jungle myna is a widespread South and Southeast Asian species found from India east to the Greater Sunda Islands, favouring forest edges, plantations, and secondary growth. It is distinguished from the common myna by its grey (not brown) plumage, tufted frontal crest, and blue-grey (not yellow) eye wattle. Like its congeners it is a highly vocal and gregarious bird, forming noisy communal roosts and foraging parties in agricultural landscapes.

children.name: Acridotheres grandis
children.description: The white-vented myna inhabits open lowlands from northeast India and Myanmar through Thailand to Indochina, commonly seen in rice paddies, grasslands, and around villages. It is characterised by its prominent crest, white undertail coverts, and extensive white wing patch visible in flight. Like the common myna it is an opportunistic forager and a common cage bird across its range, with feral populations established in parts of Southeast Asia.

children.name: Acridotheres cristatellus
children.description: The crested myna is native to southern China, Indochina, and Taiwan, and was introduced to Vancouver, Canada in the late 19th century, where a feral population persisted for nearly a century before going extinct in the early 2000s. Its prominent frontal crest and black plumage with bold white wing patches make it one of the most striking Asian mynas. A gifted vocalist and mimic, it has been a popular cage bird in China for centuries.

children.name: Acridotheres melanopterus
children.description: The black-winged myna is critically endangered and endemic to the islands of Java and Bali in Indonesia, where it was once common in open savanna and grasslands but has been devastated by mass trapping for the cage-bird trade. Unlike most mynas, its plumage is predominantly white with contrasting black wings and tail, giving it an elegant appearance that has made it extremely sought after. Captive breeding programmes are underway but wild populations have collapsed to near extinction.

children.name: Acridotheres javanicus
children.description: The Javan myna is native to Java and Bali but has been widely introduced as a cage-bird escape across Southeast Asia, becoming abundant in Singapore, Malaysia, and parts of Thailand. Nearly identical to the common myna in habits and appearance, it is distinguished by a slight tuft of feathers at the bill base and subtle plumage differences. In Singapore and peninsular Malaysia it has largely displaced the common myna through aggressive competition.

children.name: Acridotheres albocinctus
children.description: Found in the Himalayan foothills of India, Nepal, and Bhutan. A handsome black myna with a white collar and white wing patches.

children.name: Acridotheres burmannicus
children.description: A distinctive myna of Myanmar and western Thailand with a vinous-red breast and belly. Its bright orange bill and eye-patch add to its colourful appearance.

children.name: Acridotheres ater
children.description: A black myna with white wing patches from Myanmar and Thailand.

children.name: Acridotheres cinereus
children.description: A grey myna of Java and Bali, with a black head crest.

children.name: Acridotheres malayanus
children.description: A myna of the Thai-Malay peninsula and Sumatra, with a short crest and orange bill.

children.name: Gracula
children.description: Hill mynas — large, glossy black mynas of South and Southeast Asia celebrated as among the finest avian mimics in the world. The hill myna's ability to reproduce human speech with remarkable fidelity of tone, pitch, and timbre has made it the quintessential cage bird of Asia and a favourite the world over. In the wild these birds inhabit tropical forest canopy, living in small noisy flocks and producing a remarkable repertoire of loud whistles, screams, and gurgles that carry far through dense forest. Orange-yellow facial wattles and white wing patches are characteristic of the genus.

children.name: Gracula religiosa
children.description: The hill myna is perhaps the world's most celebrated talking bird, capable of reproducing human speech with a fidelity of tone and timbre that no parrot can match. Native to the foothills of the Himalayas and forest regions of South and Southeast Asia, it inhabits moist broadleaved forests from India to the Greater Sunda Islands. Wild populations have declined sharply due to trapping for the cage-bird trade and deforestation, with several island subspecies now considered threatened.

children.name: Gracula indica
children.description: The southern hill myna is found in the forests of peninsular India and Sri Lanka, recently split from Gracula religiosa on the basis of smaller size, different wattle configuration, and vocal differences. It inhabits moist evergreen and semi-evergreen forest, typically in hilly terrain, and like its relatives is a loud, conspicuous bird of the forest canopy. Populations in Sri Lanka in particular are considered vulnerable due to forest loss and persistent trapping pressure.

children.name: Gracula ptilogenys
children.description: The Sri Lanka hill myna is endemic to Sri Lanka, where it is confined to the remaining lowland rainforests of the wet zone and hills in the south and southwest of the island. It is distinguished from other hill mynas by a single pair of head wattles (rather than two) and its island-restricted range. Listed as vulnerable, it has suffered significant population declines from habitat destruction and capture for the cage-bird trade, though it remains a popular target for wildlife photographers visiting Sri Lanka.

children.name: Gracula robusta
children.description: The Nias hill myna is endemic to the island of Nias and several adjacent islands off the west coast of Sumatra, Indonesia. It is the largest member of the genus, noticeably heavier and more robust than the common hill myna, with proportionally larger wattles. Listed as endangered due to its very restricted island range, ongoing deforestation, and severe trapping pressure for both the local and international cage-bird trade.

children.name: Gracula enganensis
children.description: The Enggano hill myna is restricted to Enggano Island, a small isolated island off the southwest coast of Sumatra, Indonesia. One of the least-known members of the genus, it is considered endangered due to its tiny range, small population, and the dual threats of deforestation and trapping. Like all Gracula, it is a loud, conspicuous forest bird with glossy black plumage and orange-yellow facial wattles.

children.name: Gracula pitilogenys
children.description: Endemic to Sri Lanka's wet zone forests. Distinguished from G. indica by its shorter, less bulky yellow wattles. Listed as Endangered by habitat loss.

children.name: Gracula strenua
children.description: A large hill myna endemic to Enggano Island, Indonesia. Its massive bill and distinct vocalisations set it apart from other Gracula species.

children.name: Gracula engannoensis
children.description: A recently recognised species from Enggano Island, differing from G. religiosa in its larger size and yellow wattles covering more of the crown.

children.name: Gracula venerata
children.description: A hill myna of Flores and Alor with large yellow wattles.

children.name: Gracula picta
children.description: A hill myna of Enggano Island with a distinctive colour pattern.

children.name: Spodiopsar cineraceus
children.description: A grey starling of East Asia with white cheek patches.

children.name: Gracula temuchii
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Lamprotornis
children.description: Glossy starlings — a spectacular radiation of iridescent African starlings whose plumage reflects structural colours of breathtaking intensity: metallic blues, greens, purples, and bronzes that shift with the angle of light. These are among the most visually striking passerines on Earth, common across sub-Saharan Africa in habitats from dense forest to open savanna. Many species are familiar and abundant birds at African lodges and campsites, their liquid whistles and clanging calls among the most characteristic sounds of the African bush. Several long-tailed species are among the most ornate birds in the family.

children.name: Lamprotornis chalybaeus
children.description: The greater blue-eared starling is among the most abundant and widespread of Africa's glossy starlings, found across the entire sub-Saharan belt from Senegal to Ethiopia and south to South Africa. Its brilliant metallic blue-green plumage, offset by dark blue ear coverts and yellow-orange eye, makes it one of the continent's most striking birds. A bold, confident species frequently encountered at campsites and lodges throughout its enormous range, it forages for fruit and insects in woodland and savanna.

children.name: Lamprotornis chloropterus
children.description: The lesser blue-eared starling closely resembles its greater congener but is slightly smaller and more slender, with a purple (rather than blue-green) gloss to the underparts visible at close range. It inhabits open woodland and savanna across a broad band of sub-Saharan Africa, and the two blue-eared species frequently occur together. A common and adaptable species, it is often the first glossy starling encountered by visitors to the African bush.

children.name: Lamprotornis splendidus
children.description: The splendid starling — aptly named — is a forest-edge and riverine woodland species of West and Central Africa, its plumage an extraordinary combination of deep blue-green, violet, and iridescent bronze-green. Among the most colourful of the glossy starlings, it tends to be more secretive than savanna species, often heard before seen as it calls from high in the forest canopy. It forages primarily on fruit, playing an important role as a seed disperser in tropical forest ecosystems.

children.name: Lamprotornis superbus
children.description: The superb starling is one of the most iconic birds of East African savanna, its bold pattern of iridescent blue-green upperparts, chestnut belly, and crisp white breast band making it one of the most photographed birds in Kenya and Tanzania. Remarkably tame and confiding around lodges, camps, and Maasai villages, it is often the first African bird encountered by visitors to the region. It is a cooperative breeder, with helpers assisting dominant pairs to raise chicks.

children.name: Lamprotornis regius
children.description: The royal starling is a stunning East African endemic found in dry thorn-scrub and open woodland from southern Ethiopia and Somalia south through Kenya to northern Tanzania. The male's crown is a brilliant gold-orange, contrasting vividly with the iridescent blue-green body — a combination unique among starlings. A relatively localised species, it is nonetheless locally common in suitable habitat and is a highly sought target for birders visiting the Horn of Africa region.

children.name: Lamprotornis purpureus
children.description: The purple starling is a common West and Central African woodland and forest-edge species with predominantly purple-violet iridescent plumage and a distinctive yellow eye. It ranges across the Guinea–Congo forest zone and associated savanna-woodland mosaics, where it forages for fruit and insects in the mid and upper canopy. Like other Lamprotornis it is a gregarious species, forming flocks outside the breeding season and nesting colonially in tree cavities.

children.name: Lamprotornis corruscus
children.description: The black-bellied starling is a forest and coastal bush species of East and southern Africa, found from Kenya south along the coast to eastern South Africa. Its green-glossed upperparts contrast with dark blackish underparts, and its red eye is conspicuous at close range. A frugivorous species par excellence, it plays a vital role in dispersing seeds of coastal and forest trees throughout its range.

children.name: Lamprotornis mevesii
children.description: Meves's starling — named after the Swedish zoologist Fredrik Wilhelm Meves — is a long-tailed glossy starling of the miombo woodlands of southern and eastern Africa, from southern Tanzania south to Mozambique, Zimbabwe, and Zambia. Its extremely long, graduated purple-blue tail is its most arresting feature. A characteristic bird of the Brachystegia woodlands, it is gregarious and often seen in small flocks moving through the canopy.

children.name: Lamprotornis australis
children.description: Burchell's starling — named after the English naturalist and explorer William John Burchell — is the largest of the long-tailed glossy starlings, a bulky, impressive bird of the Limpopo valley bushveld and surrounding woodland. Common in Kruger National Park and the dry acacia woodlands of Botswana and Zimbabwe, its deep blue-purple gloss and massive tail are unmistakeable. It nests in large tree holes and is a frequent victim of nest parasitism by the greater honeyguide.

children.name: Lamprotornis nitens
children.description: The Cape starling is one of the most familiar birds of southern Africa, abundant in gardens, parks, farmland, and bushveld from southern Angola and Zambia south to the Cape. Its rich blue-green gloss and orange-red eye make it immediately recognisable. An adaptable, bold species, it readily comes to feeding stations and fruit trees in suburban gardens, and is among the most photographed African birds.

children.name: Lamprotornis hildebrandti
children.description: Hildebrandt's starling — named after the German naturalist Johann Maria Hildebrandt — is an endemic of Tanzania and southern Kenya, found in dry Acacia–Commiphora scrub and open savanna. Its combination of iridescent blue-green upperparts and vivid chestnut-orange underparts makes it one of the most beautiful starlings in Africa. It is closely related to the superb starling but allopatric with it in drier habitat, and is a cooperative breeder with helpers at the nest.

children.name: Lamprotornis shelleyi
children.description: Shelley's starling — named after the English ornithologist George Ernest Shelley — is found in dry savanna and semi-desert scrub from Ethiopia and Somalia south through Kenya and Tanzania. Its chestnut underparts and iridescent blue-green upperparts closely resemble Hildebrandt's starling, with which it was long confused; the two species are distinguished by range, head pattern, and subtle plumage differences. It is a common and conspicuous bird of the Horn of Africa and northern East Africa.

children.name: Lamprotornis pulcher
children.description: The chestnut-bellied starling is a large, distinctive species of sub-Saharan Africa's drier woodland zones from Senegal east to Ethiopia and Somalia. Its rich chestnut underparts, white iris, and iridescent blue-green upperparts form a handsome combination. A gregarious species of open Acacia savanna and thorn-scrub, it forages in flocks on the ground and in fruiting trees, and is a common and conspicuous bird across the Sahel and Horn of Africa.

children.name: Lamprotornis acuticaudus
children.description: A glossy starling of southern African savannas with a distinctive short, sharply pointed tail. Its iridescent blue-green plumage is accented by a violet belly.

children.name: Lamprotornis fischeri
children.description: A small glossy starling of East African arid bushland, from Ethiopia to Tanzania. Named after the German explorer Gustav Fischer.

children.name: Lamprotornis albicapillus
children.description: A striking starling of the Horn of Africa with a white crown and nape contrasting with its glossy blue-black body.

children.name: Lamprotornis bicolor
children.description: A black and white starling of South African grasslands.

children.name: Lamprotornis albicinctus
children.description: A glossy starling of East African highlands with a white collar.

children.name: Lamprotornis aeneus
children.description: A glossy starling of West African savannas with a bronze-green tail.

children.name: Lamprotornis cupreocaudus
children.description: A glossy starling with a copper-coloured tail, found in Central Africa.

children.name: Lamprotornis ornatus
children.description: A glossy starling endemic to the island of Principe in the Gulf of Guinea.

children.name: Gracula temuchii
children.description: A hill myna of the Sulu Archipelago in the Philippines.

children.name: Lamprotornis elisabeth
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Lamprotornis purpuroptera
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Lamprotornis caudatus
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Lamprotornis unicolor
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Lamprotornis violacior
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Lamprotornis iris
children.description: One of the most spectacular members of the genus, the Emerald Starling has brilliant iridescent green upperparts and wings with a contrasting purple crown and upper breast, creating a colour pattern unique among African starlings. A bird of the Upper Guinea forest zone — Sierra Leone, Liberia, Ivory Coast, and Ghana — it inhabits lowland rainforest and forest edge, feeding on fruit and insects in the canopy. It is rarely encountered due to the highly fragmented state of the Upper Guinea forest.

children.name: Lamprotornis chalcurus
children.description: A glossy starling of the Sahel and dry savanna from Senegal and Guinea-Bissau east through Nigeria, Chad, and Sudan to western Ethiopia, forming the northern counterpart to the more southerly blue-eared starlings. Its bronzy-purple tail (rather than blue or green) is diagnostic in the field. It inhabits dry acacia woodland, savanna, and open bushed country, foraging for fruit and large insects. Typically seen in pairs or small parties, it is less colonial than some relatives.

children.name: Lamprotornis chalcothorax
children.description: A richly coloured glossy starling of the Congo Basin rainforest and adjacent gallery forest in the Democratic Republic of Congo, the Central African Republic, and Uganda. The male's brilliant blue throat and upper breast contrast with green upperparts and a purple belly in a combination unique among glossy starlings. It inhabits the interior and edges of lowland tropical rainforest, foraging in the mid-storey and canopy for fruit and insects.

children.name: Lamprotornis sycobius
children.description: A blue-eared glossy starling of the miombo woodland of east-central Africa, from Zambia, Malawi, and Tanzania south to Mozambique and Zimbabwe. Long treated as a population of the Lesser Blue-eared Glossy Starling (L. chloropterus), it was elevated to species level based on morphological and acoustic differences. It is the characteristic glossy starling of the miombo biome, where it inhabits open Brachystegia woodland and savanna.

children.name: Lamprotornis purpuriceps
children.description: A forest-edge glossy starling of West and Central Africa, found from Sierra Leone and Guinea east through Cameroon to the Central African Republic and western DRC. Its distinctive purple or violet-blue crown and nape contrast with green body plumage and a yellow eye. It inhabits forest interior and edges, gallery forest along rivers, and secondary growth, and is less likely to be seen in open savanna than its blue-eared relatives.

children.name: Aplonis
children.description: Pacific starlings — a diverse radiation of mostly plain, dark-plumaged starlings distributed across the Indo-Pacific from South and Southeast Asia to Melanesia, Micronesia, and Polynesia. They include both widespread and abundant species and some of the most range-restricted island endemics in the avian world, with several species having gone extinct in recent centuries due to introduced predators, habitat destruction, and hunting. Their glossy black or dark greenish plumage with bright red or yellow eyes is typical of the group.

children.name: Aplonis panayensis
children.description: The Asian glossy starling is the most widespread Aplonis, found from northeast India and Bangladesh east through Southeast Asia to the Philippines, inhabiting forest edge, mangroves, parks, and gardens. Its glossy greenish-black plumage and brilliant red eye are characteristic. A highly gregarious species, it forms massive communal roosts — sometimes comprising hundreds of thousands of birds — in city parks and mangrove forests across Southeast Asia.

children.name: Aplonis metallica
children.description: The metallic starling is a highly social, migratory species that breeds colonially in far northern Australia (Cape York Peninsula) and New Guinea, building hundreds of pendulous woven nests in a single large rainforest tree. It migrates north to New Guinea and the Moluccas outside the breeding season. Its brilliant metallic green-black plumage and deep red eye make it one of the most striking Australian starlings, and its colonial nest trees are a spectacular wildlife spectacle.

children.name: Aplonis striata
children.description: The striated starling is endemic to New Caledonia and the Loyalty Islands in the southwest Pacific, one of the few Aplonis with clearly streaked (rather than uniformly glossy) underparts. It inhabits open woodland, forest edge, and cultivated areas including gardens and parks in Nouméa. A common endemic that is one of New Caledonia's most familiar birds, though not as showy as its more iridescent relatives.

children.name: Aplonis tabuensis
children.description: The Polynesian starling is among the most widespread Pacific island birds, with numerous island subspecies distributed across Tonga, Samoa, Fiji, Niue, and many other archipelagos of the southwest and central Pacific. It inhabits virtually all forest types on its island homes, from coastal coconut woodland to montane forest. A highly variable species across its range, different island populations show considerable divergence in plumage, size, and vocalisation.

children.name: Aplonis pelzelni
children.description: The Pohnpei starling — named after the Austrian ornithologist August von Pelzeln — is a critically endangered endemic of the island of Pohnpei in Micronesia. Once common, it has been reduced to a tiny population in upland forest as a result of introduced rats and black rats, which prey on eggs and nestlings. Surveys in recent decades have found very few individuals, and some authorities consider it possibly extinct; it remains one of the most critically threatened Pacific island birds.

children.name: Aplonis atrifusca
children.description: The Samoan starling is endemic to the Samoan archipelago — both independent Samoa and American Samoa — where it is one of the most familiar and abundant native forest birds. Its dark brownish-black plumage and yellow eye distinguish it from the metallic Aplonis of the western Pacific. It inhabits all forest types from sea level to the highest ridges, and feeds on fruit, insects, and nectar.

children.name: Aplonis cantoroides
children.description: The singing starling — one of New Guinea's most characteristic Aplonis — inhabits lowland and foothill forest and forest edge across New Guinea and some adjacent islands, as well as northern Cape York in Australia. Despite its name it has no particularly remarkable voice compared to other starlings; it was likely named for its continuous, varied calling. A common forest bird, it forms flocks outside breeding season and feeds on small fruits.

children.name: Aplonis magna
children.description: A distinctive Aplonis of the Biak and Numfoor islands in Cenderawasih Bay, West Papua, Indonesia, with a notably long, graduated tail that gives it a different silhouette from most of its relatives. It inhabits primary and tall secondary forest, feeding on fruit and insects in the canopy and mid-storey. Restricted to these two small islands off the northern coast of New Guinea's Bird's Head Peninsula, it is a near-threatened island endemic.

children.name: Aplonis mysolensis
children.description: A short-tailed glossy starling distributed through the Molucca Islands (Maluku) of eastern Indonesia — including Seram, Buru, the Banda Islands, and smaller satellite islands. Named after the island of Misool (historically 'Mysol') near the Bird's Head, it is a common bird of forest edge, coconut groves, and secondary scrub on the larger Moluccan islands. Its plain, glossy dark plumage and short tail distinguish it from the long-tailed and metallic Aplonis of adjacent New Guinea.

children.name: Aplonis grandis
children.description: A large, dark glossy starling restricted to San Cristobal (Makira) and Rennell islands in the Solomon Islands. It inhabits dense primary forest and forest edge, feeding on fruit and foraging in the forest canopy. As the largest member of the genus in the Solomons, it occupies a different ecological niche to the smaller co-occurring Aplonis, taking larger fruit items and ranging higher into the forest interior.

children.name: Aplonis dichroa
children.description: An Aplonis endemic to the island of Makira (San Cristobal) in the Solomon Islands, with a distinctive two-toned plumage pattern — dark above and paler below — that differentiates it from the uniformly dark A. grandis with which it shares its island. It inhabits forest interior and forest-edge vegetation at various elevations and feeds primarily on small figs and other forest fruits. The name dichroa means 'two-coloured', referring to this characteristic.

children.name: Aplonis brunneicapilla
children.description: A glossy starling of the banks of the New Hebrides archipelago (modern Vanuatu), recognised by its rusty-brown flight feathers that contrast with its otherwise dark glossy plumage. Found on the larger northern islands of the Vanuatu chain, it inhabits tropical forest and forest edge, foraging in small groups for fruit and arthropods. It is one of several island-endemic Aplonis found in island groups west of Fiji.

children.name: Aplonis corvina
children.description: The Kosrae Starling — extinct since approximately 1828 — was an Aplonis endemic to Kosrae Island (formerly Kusaie) in the Caroline Islands of Micronesia. The last specimens were collected by Russian explorers of the Seniavin expedition in 1827–29, and the species was never observed again despite subsequent visits to the island; its extinction is attributed to introduced rats and the destruction of its forest habitat. It was a medium-sized, plain dark glossy starling, similar to surviving Micronesian Aplonis.

children.name: Aplonis opaca
children.description: The most widespread Aplonis in Micronesia, occurring across a vast arc of islands from the Mariana Islands and Palau through the Federated States of Micronesia and the Marshall Islands. With nine recognised subspecies it shows considerable morphological variation across its range, and some island populations may represent distinct species. It is a bold, adaptable starling of forest, village gardens, and disturbed scrub, and is a conspicuous and familiar bird on most inhabited Micronesian islands.

children.name: Aplonis santovestris
children.description: A recently discovered Aplonis known only from a handful of specimens collected in montane forest on the island of Espiritu Santo, Vanuatu, at elevations above 1,000 m. This secretive starling inhabits dense highland forest on Vanuatu's largest island and is so rarely observed that many details of its biology, behaviour, and population size remain unknown. It is listed as Vulnerable due to its extremely small range and the threat of habitat loss from logging and agricultural expansion on Santo.

children.name: Aplonis cinerascens
children.description: A small, ashy-grey glossy starling restricted to the island of Rarotonga in the Cook Islands, the southernmost Aplonis. Unlike the typically dark-glossy members of the genus, it is notably grey-brown rather than iridescent black or green — a coloration atypical in Aplonis. It inhabits the remaining montane forest on the steep interior of Rarotonga and is listed as Vulnerable, threatened by introduced predators (rats, cats) and the limited extent of suitable native forest on the island.

children.name: Aplonis feadensis
children.description: A glossy starling of the outer Solomon Islands, occurring on the low coral atolls and remote island groups of Ontong Java, Sikaiana, Rennell, and Bellona — with the two subspecies corresponding to the northern (Ontong Java) and southern (Rennell/Bellona) island groups. It inhabits coconut forest, sea-almond, and native scrub on these flat, low-lying atolls, and is well adapted to the limited resources of atoll environments compared to its forest-interior relatives on the larger Solomon Islands.

children.name: Aplonis fusca
children.description: A small, dark starling endemic to Enggano Island — a remote island off the southwestern coast of Sumatra, Indonesia — and the adjacent Banyak Islands. It inhabits tropical forest, scrub, and the margins of village gardens on this isolated island, which has a high proportion of endemic vertebrates due to its long isolation. It is closely related to the Asian Glossy Starling but is smaller, darker, and entirely restricted to these small Indonesian islands in the Indian Ocean.

children.name: Aplonis leucostigma
children.description: A glossy starling of the Bismarck Archipelago of Papua New Guinea — New Britain, New Ireland, and associated smaller islands — with two subspecies corresponding to the larger and smaller island groups. It inhabits lowland and foothill forest and forest edge, and is one of the characteristic starlings of the Bismarck avifauna. The pale spots or streaking on the breast feathers (leucostigma = 'white mark') distinguish it from the uniformly dark Aplonis of adjacent New Guinea.

children.name: Aplonis minor
children.description: A compact, short-tailed glossy starling distributed through the Sulawesi region and adjacent Moluccan islands of Indonesia, including Sulawesi's satellite islands and parts of the Maluku chain. It is the shortest-tailed member of the genus, with a rounded tail rather than the more graduated tail of related species. Inhabiting forest edge, secondary growth, and coconut plantations, it is adaptable and common throughout its range and forms large communal roosts in village trees and bamboo groves.

children.name: Aplonis crassa
children.description: An Aplonis endemic to Rennell Island — the world's largest raised coral atoll and a UNESCO World Heritage site — in the Solomon Islands. Rennell's extreme isolation and unusual limestone terrain have produced a distinctive avifauna with several endemic species; the Rennell Starling is among the most distinctive. It inhabits the native forest of Lake Tegano's shoreline and the island's forested limestone ridges, where it forages for fruit and insects. Threatened by logging concessions and habitat conversion on this fragile island.

children.name: Onychognathus
children.description: Chestnut-winged starlings — a group of large, robust African starlings whose males are predominantly glossy blue-black or purplish with conspicuous chestnut primary patches visible in flight. The females of most species are grey-headed or paler, providing striking sexual dichromatism. They are generally birds of rocky country, cliffs, and mountains, nesting on ledges and in crevices rather than in tree cavities. The red-winged starling is a familiar and bold bird of African cities, nesting on buildings and cliff faces alike.

children.name: Onychognathus morio
children.description: The red-winged starling is one of the most familiar birds of eastern and southern Africa, equally at home on mountain cliffs and in city centres from Nairobi to Cape Town. The male is glossy blue-black with vivid chestnut primaries visible in flight; the female has a distinctive grey head. Bold and confiding around humans, it nests on building ledges, bridges, and cliff faces, and its loud, liquid call is a characteristic sound of African cities and mountains.

children.name: Onychognathus tenuirostris
children.description: The slender-billed starling inhabits montane forest and rocky ravines from Ethiopia and Eritrea south to Zimbabwe and eastern DRC, at elevations typically above 1,500 m. Its notably long, slender bill is an adaptation for extracting insects and small fruit from crevices and bark. Like other Onychognathus males are glossy black with chestnut wing panels; the slim bill immediately separates it from the similar red-winged starling.

children.name: Onychognathus walleri
children.description: Waller's starling — named after Frederick Waller, who collected the type specimen in East Africa — is a forest species of the East African highlands and the Cameroon highlands, found in montane forest from Cameroon and Nigeria east to Kenya and Tanzania. It favours dense montane forest, often near fruiting trees such as Podocarpus, and its chestnut primaries flash conspicuously in flight above the dark forest canopy.

children.name: Onychognathus nabouroup
children.description: The pale-winged starling is a bird of rocky desert and semi-desert in the arid zones of Namibia, Botswana, and the Northern Cape of South Africa — particularly the Namib Desert and the rocky gorges of the Richtersveld. Its pale, almost whitish primary panels (rather than the chestnut of most relatives) give it a distinctive flight pattern. It nests in rock crevices and cliff ledges, and is a characteristic species of the harshest desert environments in southern Africa.

children.name: Onychognathus tristramii
children.description: A bold, glossy black starling of rocky wadis, cliffs, and gorges in the Middle East and northeastern Africa, from Jordan and Israel south through the Sinai, Hejaz, and Yemen to Eritrea and Djibouti. Named after the British ornithologist Henry Baker Tristram, who collected natural history specimens throughout the Levant in the 1860s, it is notable for its tameness and commensal behaviour around human settlements, entering buildings and following trekkers through desert canyons. Males have all-black plumage with chestnut primaries; females are grey-headed.

children.name: Onychognathus salvadorii
children.description: The bristle-crowned starling is an unmistakeable bird of cliff faces, rocky gorges, and escarpments in the Ethiopian Highlands, Eritrea, and northern Somalia. Its crown is covered by stiff, elongated black bristle-like feathers that give it a uniquely shaggy-headed appearance among starlings. Named after the Italian ornithologist Tommaso Salvadori, it inhabits montane rocky terrain and was historically described from cliff colonies along the Rift Valley escarpment. A male chorus of rasping calls echoes through the gorges it inhabits.

children.name: Onychognathus blythii
children.description: A chestnut-winged starling of the dry lowlands of the Horn of Africa — Somalia, the Ogaden region of Ethiopia, and Djibouti — and the adjacent coast and islands of Yemen and Oman. Named after the English zoologist Edward Blyth, it inhabits arid scrubland, dry savanna, and rocky coastal terrain at lower elevations than its relative the Bristle-crowned Starling. It forages on the ground and in low trees for invertebrates and fruit, often near Acacia and Commiphora scrub.

children.name: Onychognathus frater
children.description: An Onychognathus endemic to the island of Socotra in the Indian Ocean — the 'Galapagos of the Indian Ocean' — and its satellites Abd al Kuri and Samha. One of Socotra's 44 endemic bird species, it inhabits the island's dramatic limestone plateaux, dragon blood tree forests, and steep granite mountains, nesting in rock crevices and cliff faces. Its isolation has produced a distinct species closely related to Tristram's Starling of the nearby Yemeni and Arabian coast.

children.name: Onychognathus albirostris
children.description: A distinctive Onychognathus of the highland cliffs, escarpments, and rocky gorges of Ethiopia and Eritrea at elevations of 1,800–4,000 m. Its pale ivory-white bill — unique among chestnut-winged starlings, all of which otherwise have dark or yellowish bills — makes it immediately identifiable. It is associated with the dramatic Afroalpine and upper montane zones of the Ethiopian Highlands, often nesting in cliffs near highland monasteries and on the faces of the Great Rift Valley's escarpment walls.

children.name: Onychognathus neumanni
children.description: A forest-associated Onychognathus of the montane forest zone in Nigeria, Cameroon, and the island of Bioko (Equatorial Guinea), named after the German ornithologist Oscar Neumann. Unlike most Onychognathus which prefer rocky terrain, Neumann's Starling inhabits the interior and edges of montane rainforest, often in the canopy of tall trees. It represents the western African component of the chestnut-winged starling radiation and is the least rock-dependent member of its genus.

children.name: Cinnyricinclus
children.description: Violet-backed starlings — small, sexually dimorphic African starlings in which males are among the most visually spectacular passerines on the continent. The male's upperparts are a dazzling, almost holographic violet-purple, reflecting light like polished metal; females and immatures are brown and streaked, so different as to appear a separate species. The violet-backed starling is a migratory woodland species that performs spectacular aerial displays; it moves seasonally across sub-Saharan Africa following rains and fruiting events.

children.name: Cinnyricinclus leucogaster
children.description: The violet-backed starling is one of Africa's most stunning birds: the male's upperparts are a vivid, iridescent violet-purple that seems almost luminescent in direct sunlight, contrasting with pure white underparts. Females are cryptically streaked brown, providing remarkable sexual dichromatism. A migratory species that follows seasonal rainfall and fruit across sub-Saharan Africa, it breeds in tree cavities in woodland and savanna and is unmistakeable wherever it occurs.

children.name: Cinnyricinclus sharpii
children.description: Sharpe's starling — named after the eminent English ornithologist Richard Bowdler Sharpe — is a montane forest specialist of the East African highlands, found in Kenya, Uganda, Rwanda, Burundi, and Tanzania at elevations of 1,500–3,000 m. Unlike the violet-backed starling it is a forest interior species, skulking in dense understorey and mid-canopy growth. Its iridescent violet-purple plumage is present in both sexes, though duller in the female.

children.name: Cinnyricinclus femoralis
children.description: Abbott's starling — named after the American physician and naturalist William Louis Abbott — is a rare and localised montane forest bird endemic to the highlands of Kenya and northeast Tanzania. It inhabits the forest interior at 1,800–2,500 m elevation in a small number of highland forest fragments. Assessed as vulnerable due to its tiny range and continuing forest loss from agriculture and charcoal production, it is one of East Africa's most range-restricted endemic birds.

children.name: Speculipastor
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the magpie starling, a striking black-and-white starling of arid East Africa. With its pied pattern and long tail it superficially recalls a magpie, hence its English name. It inhabits dry open country and Acacia–Commiphora scrubland from Ethiopia and Somalia south through Kenya, nesting in termite mounds or tree cavities.

children.name: Speculipastor bicolor
children.description: The magpie starling is a striking black-and-white starling of arid East Africa, its pied plumage quite unlike any other member of the family. Restricted to dry Acacia–Commiphora scrubland and open thorn-bush from Ethiopia and Somalia south to northern Tanzania, it tends to be locally nomadic, following rainfall and fruiting events across the semi-arid zone. Males are contrastingly black and white; females have brownish-grey replacing the male's black areas.

children.name: Creatophora
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the wattled starling, a remarkable African starling known for the extraordinary seasonal transformation of breeding males. Outside the breeding season the wattled starling is a plain, greyish-brown bird, but during breeding males develop highly conspicuous bright yellow and black wattles on the head and become partially bald, one of the most dramatic breeding adornments among starlings. It is a nomadic, locust-following species of open grasslands and savanna across sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Creatophora cinerea
children.description: The wattled starling is one of the most nomadic of all African birds, forming immense wandering flocks that follow locust swarms across sub-Saharan Africa. In breeding plumage males develop vivid yellow and black head wattles and become partially bald — a transformation so dramatic that early ornithologists described breeding and non-breeding birds as different species. They breed colonially, with hundreds of pairs nesting simultaneously when locusts are abundant, often finishing the entire breeding cycle within weeks.

children.name: Saroglossa
children.description: A monotypic Asian genus containing the spot-winged starling, a medium-sized starling of the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia. Males have distinctive rufous underparts and a spotted wing pattern; females are duller. It inhabits broadleaved forest and forest edge, often visiting flowering trees for nectar and small fruit.

children.name: Saroglossa spiloptera
children.description: The spot-winged starling inhabits broadleaved forest and forest edge from the Himalayan foothills through northeast India, Myanmar, and Thailand to Indochina. The male's rufous-orange underparts and spotted wing panel make it one of the more distinctively patterned Asian starlings. It is an altitudinal migrant, descending from the hills in winter; at flowering and fruiting trees it sometimes gathers in small flocks with other starlings and barbets.

children.name: Scissirostrum
children.description: A monotypic Indonesian genus containing the finch-billed starling (or grosbeak starling), notable for its unusually heavy, finch-like bill that is quite unlike the slender bill of most starlings. It is endemic to Sulawesi and adjacent islands, where it is a colonial breeder excavating cavities in dead wood. It feeds heavily on seeds, using its robust bill to dehusk tough grain — an unusual dietary specialisation among starlings.

children.name: Scissirostrum dubium
children.description: The finch-billed starling is endemic to Sulawesi and its satellite islands in Indonesia, where it inhabits open woodland, forest edge, and cultivated areas including rice paddies. Its stout, conical bill is unique among starlings and reflects a dietary shift toward hard seeds, though it also takes fruit and insects. A highly colonial breeder, it excavates nesting holes in dead tree trunks and stumps, sometimes forming colonies of dozens of pairs.

children.name: Mino
children.description: Yellow-faced mynas — large, colourful mynas of New Guinea and adjacent islands with vivid bare yellow facial skin and bold black-and-yellow or black-and-orange plumage. They are among the most conspicuous birds of New Guinea's forest canopy, their loud calls carrying far through the dense vegetation. Like the hill mynas of Gracula, they are celebrated for their vocal abilities and are popular cage birds across Southeast Asia and in aviculture worldwide.

children.name: Mino anais
children.description: The golden myna is distributed across the Moluccas and western New Guinea (Vogelkop), where it inhabits primary and secondary forest and forest edge. Its rich golden-yellow body with black wings and tail, and bare yellow facial skin, make it one of the most strikingly coloured mynas. A fruit-eating species of the forest canopy, it is considered vulnerable in some parts of its range due to trapping for the cage-bird trade.

children.name: Mino dumontii
children.description: The yellow-faced myna — named after the French explorer Jules-Sébastien-César Dumont d'Urville — is distributed across New Guinea and extreme northern Australia (Cape York), inhabiting forest, forest edge, and tall secondary growth. It is the most widespread Mino, with its vivid yellow face patch, red-orange bill, and black-and-white body forming a striking combination. A bold, noisy species of the forest canopy, it is frequently kept as a cage bird throughout the region.

children.name: Mino kreffti
children.description: The long-tailed myna — named after the German-Australian naturalist Johann Ludwig Gerard Krefft — is a large Mino distributed across the Bismarck Archipelago, Solomon Islands, and adjacent island groups of Melanesia. As the name suggests, it has a notably longer tail than other Mino species, giving it a more elegant silhouette. It inhabits primary and tall secondary forest on these large tropical islands, and is a conspicuous bird of the forest canopy.

children.name: Ampeliceps
children.description: A monotypic Asian genus containing the golden-crested myna, a striking medium-sized myna of mainland Southeast Asia. The male's brilliant golden-yellow crown and facial patches contrast vividly with its otherwise black plumage, making it one of the most immediately recognisable mynas of the region. It inhabits moist broadleaved forest and forest edge from northeast India through Myanmar and Thailand to Indochina.

children.name: Ampeliceps coronatus
children.description: The golden-crested myna is a spectacular inhabitant of moist broadleaved forest and forest edge from northeast India (Assam) through Myanmar, Thailand, and Cambodia to Vietnam. The male's gold crown, yellow cheek patches, and yellow wing patches flash brilliantly against otherwise glossy black plumage. A mid-canopy forager taking fruit, berries, and large insects, it is typically encountered in pairs or small groups in tall forest.

children.name: Leucopsar
children.description: Pure white starlings with striking bare blue facial skin; one of the most endangered birds in the world.

children.name: Leucopsar rothschildi
children.description: Nearly extinct in the wild, with fewer than 100 individuals remaining on Bali. Its pure white plumage, blue eye-patch, and elegant crest make it one of the most beautiful of all starlings. Named after Lord Rothschild.

children.name: Poeoptera
children.description: Narrow-tailed starlings — a group of small, slender African forest starlings with pointed tails and subtly glossed dark plumage. They inhabit the montane and highland forests of East and Central Africa, moving in active flocks through the forest canopy. Though less brilliantly coloured than Lamprotornis, they have their own iridescent sheen — bronzy-green or purplish depending on light — and their graceful build and agile flight set them apart from the more robust glossy starlings.

children.name: Poeoptera lugubris
children.description: A slender, dark starling of the forest zone from Nigeria east through Cameroon, the Central African Republic, and the Congo Basin. It inhabits lowland rainforest and forest edge, moving in small flocks through the canopy and feeding on fruit and insects. Its narrow, pointed tail and slim build are distinctive; in flight it resembles a swift more than a typical starling. The name lugubris ('mournful') refers to its sombre, nearly black plumage with a faint bronzy-green iridescence.

children.name: Poeoptera kenricki
children.description: A narrow-tailed starling of the highland forest zone of Tanzania and Kenya, including the Usambara, Uluguru, and Udzungwa mountains. Named after Philip John Kenrick, who collected birds in East Africa during British colonial expeditions in the early 20th century, it inhabits montane forest and forest edge at elevations of 900–2,400 m. It forms small active flocks in the upper canopy and is associated with fruiting fig trees. An Eastern African Rift endemic and one of the rarer members of the genus.

children.name: Poeoptera stuhlmanni
children.description: A narrow-tailed starling of the montane forest of the Albertine Rift — the Ruwenzori Mountains, the Virunga Volcanoes, and adjacent highland forests of Uganda, Rwanda, Burundi, and eastern DRC. Named after the German naturalist Franz Ludwig Stuhlmann, who joined the Emin Pasha Relief Expedition across East Africa in 1890-92, it inhabits the interior of montane forest from 1,200 to 2,600 m. It is associated with fruiting trees and moves in fast-flying flocks through the forest interior.

children.name: Poeoptera femoralis
children.description: A narrow-tailed starling of the highland forest of Kenya and northern Tanzania, restricted to a small number of forest patches including the Kikuyu Escarpment, Mt Kenya foothills, and the Taita Hills. The male has glossy blue-black plumage with a bright white belly — an unusual pattern for the genus. Vulnerable due to its highly fragmented forest habitat and small total population, it moves through the upper canopy in small groups and feeds predominantly on mistletoe berries and insects.

children.name: Poeoptera sharpii
children.description: A narrow-tailed starling of the highland forest of the Rwenzori Mountains and adjacent highland forests of Uganda and the eastern DRC, named after the prolific British ornithologist and cataloguer Richard Bowdler Sharpe. It inhabits primary montane forest at elevations of 1,500–2,800 m, moving in small fast-flying flocks through the forest canopy. Distinguished from Stuhlmann's Starling (P. stuhlmanni) by subtly different plumage tones and vocalisations; both species can be found in the same Albertine Rift forests.

children.name: Basilornis
children.description: Helmet mynas — a group of large, spectacular mynas found on the islands of Sulawesi and the Philippines, characterised by prominent bare skin wattles, crests, or casques on the head that give the genus its name ('king bird'). They are birds of primary tropical forest, feeding on large fruit, insects, and small vertebrates in the upper canopy. Their large size, bold black plumage, and ornate head appendages make them among the most distinctive members of the starling family.

children.name: Basilornis celebensis
children.description: A large, glossy black myna with a striking, helmet-like bare yellow casque on the crown, endemic to Sulawesi (Celebes) and its satellite islands. It inhabits primary lowland and montane forest, foraging in the canopy and at fruiting trees for large fruits, insects, and occasional small vertebrates. Despite being conspicuous and noisy, it is increasingly scarce due to widespread deforestation and forest conversion on Sulawesi. It is the most distinctive member of the Sulawesian starling community.

children.name: Basilornis galeatus
children.description: A helmet myna endemic to the Banggai and Sula Islands off the east coast of Sulawesi, Indonesia. It has a prominent, fleshy orange-yellow galeate (helmet-shaped) crest on the crown that gives it its name. Restricted to these small island groups, it inhabits lowland primary forest and is threatened by deforestation for agricultural conversion. Very little is known of its ecology, and it is rarely photographed or studied due to the remoteness of its island habitat.

children.name: Basilornis corythaix
children.description: A helmet myna of Seram (Ceram) Island in the Moluccas, eastern Indonesia, with a notably long, recurved crest of bare orange skin on the forehead. It is the Moluccan representative of the helmet myna radiation, inhabiting primary lowland and hill forest of this large Wallacean island. The species name corythaix is from the Greek for helmet — reflecting the elaborate crest. Little studied and rarely encountered, it is threatened by forest clearance for agriculture and hunting.

children.name: Basilornis mirandus
children.description: A helmet myna of montane forest on the island of Mindanao in the Philippines, particularly associated with the forests around Mount Apo — the Philippines' highest peak and a UNESCO World Heritage site. Glossy black with a bare orange facial patch and frontal wattle, it inhabits forest from 900 to 2,500 m elevation. Like other Basilornis it takes large fruits in the upper canopy and plays an important role as a seed disperser for large-fruited forest trees. Threatened by continued forest clearance on Mindanao.

children.name: Streptocitta
children.description: Magpie starlings — two striking black-and-white starlings of Sulawesi with long, graduated tails that give them a magpie-like silhouette. They are noisy, conspicuous birds of the forest canopy and forest edge, travelling in small parties and calling loudly with a variety of harsh and musical notes. Their pattern of glossy black upperparts, white underparts, and long pied tail is unique in the Sturnidae and makes them immediately recognisable members of the Sulawesian avifauna.

children.name: Streptocitta albicollis
children.description: The more widespread of the two Streptocitta, found across mainland Sulawesi and some adjacent small islands, inhabiting primary and mature secondary forest from the lowlands to about 1,500 m. A bold, noisy bird of forest edges, riverine forest, and tall secondary growth, its long tail and white neck form an unmistakeable silhouette. It forages in the forest mid-storey and canopy for large insects, geckos, and fruit, moving in pairs or small family groups and giving loud rattling calls.

children.name: Streptocitta albertinae
children.description: Restricted to the Sula Islands (Sulabesi, Mangole, and Taliabu) east of the Sulawesi mainland, this Streptocitta differs from its relative in having prominent bare yellow skin around the eye rather than feathered orbital skin. Named after Princess Albertina of the Netherlands, it inhabits primary lowland forest and forest edge on these rarely visited islands. Little studied, it is classified as Vulnerable due to its small range and the ongoing deforestation of the Sula archipelago for logging and coconut plantations.

children.name: Sarcops
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Coleto — one of the most distinctive and bizarre members of the starling family, endemic to the Philippine Islands. The Coleto has a large, almost entirely naked and brightly coloured head with a small tuft of feathers remaining at the crown — an extreme bare-skin display found in no other starling. Its gregarious, noisy behaviour in fruiting trees and its odd, naked-headed appearance make it one of the most recognisable birds in the Philippine forest.

children.name: Sarcops calvus
children.description: The Coleto is a common and conspicuous bird of lowland and montane forest across the Philippine Islands, instantly recognisable by its almost entirely naked, bright pink head contrasting with glossy black and white body plumage. The bare head skin is thought to dissipate heat — similar to the naked heads of some vultures — and plays a role in social signalling. It is gregarious, gathering in large noisy groups at fruiting trees, and is an important seed disperser of large forest fruits including figs and wild nutmegs. Its loud, varied calls fill Philippine forest at dawn.

children.name: Enodes
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Fiery-browed Myna — an extraordinary starling of Sulawesi in which the male has brilliant orange-red superciliary plumes above the eye that flash like sparks in the forest light. Unlike most mynas, which are open-country or forest-edge birds, the Fiery-browed Myna is a bird of primary forest interior, moving through the canopy in small groups and feeding on insects and fruit. It is considered a secondary-cavity nester, using old woodpecker holes in large trees.

children.name: Enodes erythrophris
children.description: A spectacular endemic of Sulawesi, Indonesia, in which the male has vivid, elongated orange-red superciliary feathers that project over and beyond the eye — a unique feature among the world's starlings. Its glossy black plumage makes the fiery superciliaries even more striking in the forest light. Found from lowland to montane forest up to 1,800 m on mainland Sulawesi and some offshore islands, it is a frugivore and insectivore of primary forest and is threatened by the ongoing loss of primary forest on Sulawesi.
